coredata.dc:title: Achieving red room temperature afterglow carbon dots in composite matrices through chromophore conjugation degree controlling
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Jing
Yi, Zhenzhao
Ye, Yunxia
Ren, Xudong
Li, Qijun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as new purely organic afterglow materials, show great potential applications in many fields, but efficient red room temperature afterglow (RTA) CDs are very scarce due to lack of effective synthetic method and blur RTA mechanism. Herein, an easy, high-output synthesis strategy for in-situ preparation of red RTA CD-based composites is developed through one-step microwave radiating large conjugated structure precursor. The systematic research shows that N, O-doped large conjugated structures on CD surfaces are responsible for the red RTA emission in view of their effectively reducing the band gap between the excited and ground state as well as creating afterglow center. Meanwhile the in-situ synthetic method makes CDs to be tightly embedded into the matrices by robust chemical covalent and multiple hydrogen bonding interactions, thus, greatly suppressing non-radiative transitions. Based on the above theoretical guidance, multi-color (green, yellow-green and red) RTA CDs-based materials were successfully prepared by controlled the conjugation degree of chromophore. In addition, the CD-based composites have potential applications in light-emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting. This work could provide an effective strategy to prepare red RTP materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Red room temperature afterglow
Conjugated structure
In-situ
Light-emitting diode
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the RTA CD-based composites
Preparation and micromorphology of rCDs and rCD-powders
Structural characterization of rCDs and rCD-powders

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
Natural Science Research of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for in-situ synthesis rCD-powders.

(a) TEM images of rCD; inset: HRTEM image of rCD. (b) Digital photographs of rCD-powders under daylight, 365 nm UV lamp, and the removal of 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, respectively. (c) and (d) FL and afterglow emission spectra of rCD-powders under different excitation wavelengths, respectively. (e) Afterglow decay curve and lifetimes of rCD-powders monitored at 605 nm. (f) Afterglow emission spectra of rCD-powders at 298 and 77 K, under 365 nm excitation.

(a) Deconvolution of high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of rCDs. (b) FTIR spectra of rCDs and the microwave products of urea, respectively. (c) Afterglow excitation spectrum of rCD-powders and absorption spectra of gCD-powders, yCD-powders and rCD-powders, respectively. (d) Afterglow emission spectra of gCD-powders, yCD-powders and rCD-powders, respectively.
Proposed mechanism for the long wavelength RTA emission of CD-based composites.

(a) and (b) emission spectrum of the LEDs with InGaN UV (365 nm) and blue (450 nm) respectively. (c) Calculated PL emission color coordinates in the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. (d) Graphic color anti-counterfeiting and discoloration display. (f) Information encryption. The patterned security feature of “jiang da” was mixed by rCD-powders and orange fluorescent powder, while “su xue” was only filled with orange fluorescent powders.

head.ce:title: Achieving red room temperature afterglow carbon dots in composite matrices through chromophore conjugation degree controlling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Micro-nano Optoelectronics and Terahertz Technology, School of Mechanical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Micro-nano Optoelectronics and Terahertz Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Micro-nano Optoelectronics and Terahertz Technology, School of Mechanical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Baosheng Group Co.Ltd, No.1 Road Suzhong, Baoying County, Yangzhou, 225800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Baosheng Group Co.Ltd, No.1 Road Suzhong, Baoying County, Yangzhou, 225800, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as new purely organic afterglow materials, show great potential applications in many fields, but efficient red room temperature afterglow (RTA) CDs are very scarce due to lack of effective synthetic method and blur RTA mechanism. Herein, an easy, high-output synthesis strategy for in-situ preparation of red RTA CD-based composites is developed through one-step microwave radiating large conjugated structure precursor. The systematic research shows that N, O-doped large conjugated structures on CD surfaces are responsible for the red RTA emission in view of their effectively reducing the band gap between the excited and ground state as well as creating afterglow center. Meanwhile the in-situ synthetic method makes CDs to be tightly embedded into the matrices by robust chemical covalent and multiple hydrogen bonding interactions, thus, greatly suppressing non-radiative transitions. Based on the above theoretical guidance, multi-color (green, yellow-green and red) RTA CDs-based materials were successfully prepared by controlled the conjugation degree of chromophore. In addition, the CD-based composites have potential applications in light-emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting. This work could provide an effective strategy to prepare red RTP materials.

Multicolor (green, yellow-green and red) RTA CD-based composites are successfully prepared by controlled the conjugation degree of chromophore.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials have received much attention in biological imaging, optoelectronic display and lighting and security systems because of their long lifetimes and large Stokes shifts [1–5]. Carbon dots (CDs), as new purely organic RTA materials, have been a rapidly growing research area due to their advantages of low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, great stability, and simple synthesis routes [6–12]. To obtain efficient RTA features of CDs, the researchers are mainly based on the following methods: i) incorporating O, N, P and B atoms with lone-pair electrons on the surface of CDs to promote the inefficient intersystem crossing (ISC) for effectively populating triplet excitons; ii) embedding CDs into a variety of matrices to restrain the nonradiative decay rate of the triplet excitons [13–21]. Recently, our group have reported a series of afterglow CD-based composites through doping N and O atoms in CD surfaces and embedding CDs in solid matrices (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol, polyurethane, recrystallized urea/biuret and boric acid) [22–26]. However, most CDs show afterglow emission in the green to yellow-green regions only. Though few reports have just mentioned long-wavelength RTA of CDs, they provide no detailed information about the RTA mechanism [15,27–29]. In addition, their synthetic methods usually suffer from low yield, multiple synthesis steps and time-consuming (several hours). Thus, it is urgent need to develop an easy, high-output and efficient synthesis strategy and theory to prepare orange or red RTA emissive CDs.

It is known that the emission wavelength of afterglow is related to the energy gap between the excited and ground state. Increasing the conjugated degree of luminescent molecules usually can reduce the energy gap, resulting in a red shift of the emission wavelength. For the organic molecule system, Bolton and co-workers obtained orange-emitting phosphorescence materials by increasing the conjugation length of the chromophore [30]. The afterglow of CDs mainly originates from molecule state (e.g., aromatic carbonyls and N-heterocycles on the surfaces of CDs) [12,13], which is similar to the organic molecule. In theory, choosing N, O-doped precursors with large conjugated system to synthesize CDs can not only form potential afterglow centers on the CD surfaces, but also retain the inherent conjugated structure [31]. Therefore, this strategy might be a feasible method to synthesize orange or red RTA emissive CDs.

In this work, we developed an effective method and theory to prepare red room RTA CD-based composites through one-step microwave radiating precursor with large conjugated structure. The introduction of the large conjugated structure and N, O heteroatoms from precursors not only facilitates to ISC, but also can reduce the energy gap between the excited and ground state. Meanwhile during the reaction process, the CDs formed and were in-situ embedded into composite matrices (the microwave products of urea) where strong robust chemical covalent and multiple hydrogen bonding could greatly surpass the non-radiative transition of the excited triplet states. As anticipate, the prepared CD-based composites show a red afterglow emission with long average lifetime of 211 ms, which is higher than that of most reported red RTA CD materials. Meanwhile they display a broad orange fluorescent emission (half peak width up to 137 nm) and a high quantum yield of 30%. Based on the above theoretical guidance, multi-color (green, yellow-green and red) RTA CDs-based materials were successfully prepared by controlled the conjugation degree of chromophore. Based on these excellent performances, the obtained CD-based composites were applied to light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the RTA CD-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: Alizarin (0.005 g) and urea (20 g) were added into a beaker. Then, red RTA emissive CD-based composites (rCD-powders) were prepared under a domestic 750 W microwave oven for 4–5 min. During the irradiation, alizarin dissolved in melted urea and formed a purple transparent solution, then the solution changed from purple to pink and finally formed pink solid, indicating the formation of rCD-powders. The final product, over 10 g of rCD-powders, was obtained by grinding dried blocks in agate morta. The obtained powders were dissolved in ethanol, centrifuged to remove the large particles, and then dialyzed with ethanol through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 48 h. The final product (rCDs) was used to analysis and characterization. The green RTA emissive CD-based composites (gCD-powders) and the yellow RTA emissive CD-based composites (yCD-powders) were prepared under the same method as rCD-powders.
rCD-powders were encased in superglue and coated on a UV (365 nm) and blue (450 nm) LED chips. The chips were hardened for 2 h at 80 °C, forming a homogeneous color-conversion layer.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded on a H800 instrument (Hitachi, Japan) and a HRTEM JEOL 2100 system operating at 200 kV (Japan), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed by using an ESCALAB 250 XPS system (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was collected on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer (Germany). The absorbance spectra of CDs and CD-based composites was recorded by using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer (Japan). The photoluminescence and afterglow spectra, absolute QY, and afterglow lifetime of the CDs and CD-based composites were measured using Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan), Edinburgh FS5 and FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (UK). LEDs (3w) was used to provide white light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and micromorphology of rCDs and rCD-powders
ce:section.ce:para: The rCD-powders were synthesized by one-step microwave radiating alizarin and urea (Fig. 1 , the selection of alizarin is mainly due to its large conjugated structure). By this simple approach, we could easily prepare more than 10 g of rCD-powders in one pot, where the reaction time to form rCD-powders is less than 5 min (Fig. S1). This easy, high-output synthesis strategy is important for their industrial-scale application. The morphologies of the obtained rCDs were characterized using TEM. TEM images of the rCDs demonstrate the formation of well-dispersed nanoparticles with a mean particle diameter of ≈3.7 nm (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S2a). The HRTEM image shows that the rCDs have visible crystalline lattice fringes (Fig. S2b and inset of Fig. 2a). The lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the graphene (100) planes [32,33].

The rCD-powders display an orange fluorescence emission under UV (365 nm) excitation. Interestingly, after photoexcitation was stopped, a red afterglow was observed through the naked eye at room temperature (Fig. 2b). Surprisingly, even if the excitation light was replaced by white light, a red afterglow emission could still be observed (Fig. S3). This unique phenomenon prompted us to further study their photophysical properties. A photoluminescence (PL) property study reveals that rCD-powders exhibit an excitation-independent PL with three emission bands at about 520, 560, and 600 nm (Fig. 2c). Similar to PL behavior, the afterglow spectra of rCD-powders also show an excitation-independent afterglow with a main emission peak at ~605 nm (Fig. 2d). Such wide excitation feature is believed to be responsible for the observed red afterglow emission under white light excitation. The time-resolved decay spectra were carried out, and the average lifetimes were calculated to be 137 and 211 ms under 365 and 450 nm excitation, respectively (Fig. 2e and Table S1), which is far surpass the reported red CD materials so far [27]. Such long afterglow lifetime suggest that triplet electrons are involved. In order to investigate the nature of triplet relevant process, the afterglow spectrum of CD-powders was detected at low temperature (77 K). Fig. 2f shows that the rCD-powders at 77 K displays main emission peak at 605 nm, and the afterglow intensity is as similar as that of RTA spectrum. These results imply that observed red emission at room temperature may be originated from the co-existence of phosphorescence and thermal active delayed fluorescence (TADF). As we all known, phosphorescence usually shows a significant increase as the temperature decrease while TADF has an opposite trend [34,35]. This positive and negative synergy leads to almost unchanged intensity at 77 K compared to room temperature (298 K). In addition, afterglow spectrum of CD powders at 373 K was carried out (Fig. S4). The result shows that the afterglow intensity at 605 nm exhibits a reduction at 373 K compared to that of afterglow spectrum at 298 K, implying TADF represents a small contribution for the observed red afterglow of rCD-powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of rCDs and rCD-powders
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the origin of red afterglow, the photophysical properties of rCDs and rCD-powders were investigated. The formation and chemical structure of rCDs and rCD-powders were studied by the XPS and FTIR. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) shows three peaks at 283.3, 284.9 and 287.9 eV for C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO/CN, respectively, suggesting that the rCDs surface is rich in conjugated aromatic carbonyl and N-heterocycle. XPS spectra of rCDs and the microwave products of urea were compared to reveal formation mechanism of rCD-powders. The rCDs mainly contain C, N and O elements with corresponding contents to be 52.03%, 25.44% and 22.54%, respectively, while the corresponding elements in the microwave products of urea are 36.10%, 39.00%, and 24.83%, respectively. Because the atomic concentrations of C and N elements in urea are almost equal, while in rCDs the content of nitrogen is far less than that of carbon, herein urea is mainly used as nitrogen source while alizarin serves as major carbon source. In addition, the nitrogen content in the microwave products of urea is far less than that of urea, which indicates the self-condensation reaction should have occurred (During the microwave radiation process, urea self-polymerized and formed the mixture of biuret, cyanuric acid, ammelide and ammeline). These XPS results were further confirmed by the FTIR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3b, FTIR spectrum of rCDs displays the stretching vibration for CC (1604 cm−1), indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures. The bands centered at 3432, 1706 and 1459 cm−1 demonstrate the existence of N–H, CO, and C–N groups, respectively, which is similar as that of the microwave products of urea (Fig. 3b). The similar characteristic peaks show that the surface of CDs is modified by abundant nongraphitizing matrixes chains. According to the above discussion, a possible growth mechanism for the rCD-powders is proposed. The carbonization reactions happened between urea and alizarin to form rCDs with large N, O-doped conjugated structure and abundant nongraphitizing matrix chains on their surfaces. Meanwhile, the self-polymerization of excess urea also happened in this condition. Finally, the rCDs were in-situ embedded into the composite matrices to form rCD-powders.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of rCD-powders exhibited several absorption bands at 225 and 400–600 nm (Fig. 3c), which were assigned to the π→π* transition of the aromatic CC bond, and n→π* transitions of the large conjugate system containing CO and CN bonds, respectively [36]. The RTA excitation (RTAE) spectrum was measured under 605 nm emission. The RTAE spectrum of rCD-powders exhibits a broad peak at 400–550 nm (Fig. 3c), which is accordance with the absorption band of the large conjugate system containing CO and CN bonds. Therefore, combined with FTIR, XPS, and UV results, we conclude that the observed red afterglow emission of rCD-powders may originate from the N, O-doped large conjugated structure on CDs surface, meanwhile the in-situ formed CDs were firmly fixed by robust chemical covalent and multiple hydrogen bonding interactions (no red afterglow emission was observed from the physical blends of the rCDs and the pyrolysis product of urea.), thus, greatly suppressing non-radiative transitions. from the physical blends of the HN-CDs and urea.

To further explore the conjugated effect on red afterglow of CDs, urea and monomers (2-Hydroxy-1,4-naphthoquinone and 2,5-Dihydroxy-1,4-benzoquinone, the structural formulas are shown in Fig. S5) with different conjugation degrees were selected to carry out the contrast experiments. It should be noticed that the chromophore conjugation structures on CD surfaces mainly depend on the conjugated degree of precursor. During the CDs formation, the original structure of the reaction precursors usually retains on the surfaces of CDs. Structurally, the benzoquinone and naphthoquinone moieties are comparable to the anthraquinone moiety found in alizarin, but they have less extensive conjugation. All the synthesis conditions were kept constant to ensure effective comparison and the corresponding products were named as gCD-powders and yCD-powders. TEM images of the gCD and yCD demonstrate the formation of well-dispersed CDs (Fig. S6). The UV–vis absorption spectrum indicates that of gCD-powders and yCD-powders have no obvious absorption bands at rang 400–600 nm (Fig. 3c) compared to that of rCDs. The gCD-powders and yCD-powders display blue fluorescence emission under UV (365 nm) excitation (Fig. S7); after photoexcitation was stopped, green and yellow-green afterglow were observed through the naked eye at room temperature, respectively (Fig. S7). The yCD-powders had a maximum emission wavelength of 535 nm whereas the maximum emission wavelength of the gCD-powders was centered at 493 nm under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 3d). As the degree of conjugation increases, the afterglow emission wavelength has a red shift from green light to red light region. It is confirmed again that large conjugated structures play an important role in arriving at CDs with long wavelength RTA emission. A possible mechanism for the long wavelength RTA emission is illustrated in Fig. 4 . As the degree of conjugation increases, it can effectively reduce energy gap between the excited and ground state, resulting in long wavelength RTA emission.

The QYs of rCD-powders synthesized at various alizarin contents were also recorded (Fig. S8a). The absolute QY were measured to be 30% at low concentration, which is far surpass the reported orange CD composite materials so far. In addition, the PL spectrum of rCD-powders shows a broad fluorescence emission (half peak width up to 120 nm) under 365 nm excitation, which shows great advantage for the white LEDs over quantum dots with narrow emission. The intensity of PL and QY are almost unchanged at low concentrations, but they gradually decrease with an increase in alizarin content. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to more CDs being produced at high concentration, leading to an increase in the level of self-absorption of CDs [37].

A photostability experiment with the rCD-powders reveals no obvious photobleaching as expected by repeating excitation illumination at 365 nm for 30 min (Fig. S8b). In a further set of experiments, we investigated the effects of temperature on the PL of rCD-powders. The rCD-powders shows a slight fluorescence reduction at 373 K compared to 298 K (Fig. S9), and they retain more than 90% of PL intensity after treatment at 373 K for 2 h, indicating a good thermal stability. In addition, we investigated the effects of water on the optical properties of rCD-powders (Figs. S8c and d). The intensity of afterglow in the presence of water is more than twice than that of the dry rCD-powders. This increased afterglow in the presence of water can be explained that the highly ordered bound water can construct robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks between the rCDs and the matrices, which not only effectively rigidifies phosphorescence emissive species of the CDs but also greatly enhances the rigidity of the entire system [24]. While almost identical PL curves were obtained in the presence and absence of water, revealing a good resistance toward moisture.

Based on the advantageous properties of rCD-powders demonstrated above, including broad and efficient red emission feature, and high resistance toward UV light, thermal and moisture, they were prepared and employed as the color conversion layer to fabricate LED prototypes [38–41]. As shown in Fig. 5 c, the coordinate of the LED is located at (0.55, 0.42) by combining rCD-powders with UV (365 nm) LED chips (the emission spectrum of as-fabricated LEDs are shown in Fig. 5a). Furthermore, the rCD-powders have a broad PL emission with a high absolute QY of 28% under the 450 nm excitation and the FWHM can reach up to the record of 137 nm, which make it possible to emit white light (0.33, 0.22) with InGaN blue (450 nm, the emission spectrum of as-fabricated LEDs are shown in Fig. 5b). The color rendering index and luminous efficacy are 60 and 3.1%, respectively. In addition, the CD-powders with distinguishable lifetime codes and the multicolor afterglow emissions could be developed for promising applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting and discoloration display. To demonstrate such applications, a simple pattern of a flower was fabricated with different phosphors (Fig. 5d). As shown in Fig. 5d, it exhibited an orange “petal”, and blue “stamen” and “leaves” under 365 nm UV-lamp. A red “petal”, yellow-green “stamen” and green “leaves” were observed by camera after removing the light source. These excellent properties make it possible to apply in graphic color anti-counterfeiting and discoloration display for entertainments. Furthermore, the application of rCD-powders in information encryption has also been proved in Fig. 5e. Under 365 nm excitation, the school badge pattern of “jiang su da xue” is visible and exhibits orange PL colors. Once the excitation was turned off, the red RTA emission “jiang da” could be recognized from “jiang su da xue”.

In summary, red afterglow emission CD-based composites were successfully prepared by a one-pot microwave radiating method. The strongest afterglow emission of rCD-powders was observed centered at 605 nm with average lifetime of 211 ms and QY as high as ≈30%. The study results demonstrate the red afterglow emission is synergistically contributed to the N, O-doped large conjugated structure of the surfaces of CDs, which is originated from the selection of large conjugated precursors. Meanwhile, the self-condensation products of urea form a large hydrogen-bonded networks, where CDs are firmly fixed by robust chemical covalent and multiple hydrogen bonding interactions, thus, greatly suppressing non-radiative transitions. Furthermore, the efficient long-wavelength fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-emissive CD-based composites demonstrate the potential applications in LED and anti-counterfeiting fields. The design concept presented in this work would provide a novel route for achieving long-wavelength afterglow emission CDs.

Jing Tan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Project administration. Zhenzhao Yi: Validation, Formal analysis. Yunxia Ye: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Xudong Ren: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Qijun Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (BK20190865, BK20190864), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019M651726), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (19KJB430014), Jiangsu Provincial Program for High-Level Innovative and Entrepreneurial Talents Introduction and the High-tech Research Key laboratory of Zhenjiang (SS2018002). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Yuhua Zhu at Nanjing University for allowing us accessing the fluorescence spectrophotometer in her lab.
